era_context: Alexander continues his conquest of the Persian Achaemenid Empire, consolidating his control of the Levant and preparing to invade Egypt. This year sees the completion of the siege of Tyre and Alexander's invasion of Egypt, where he is recognized as pharaoh and the son of the god Amun. The Persian Empire is weakening, though Darius III remains at large and will attempt to mount a final resistance. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin consolidating power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (contemporary but no synchronization with Old World chronology)

events.title: Completion of the siege of Tyre; city destroyed
events.description: After a siege lasting approximately seven months (beginning in 333 BCE), Alexander finally captures the city of Tyre. The siege is one of the most famous in antiquity, as Alexander constructs a causeway from the mainland to the island to allow his siege engines to reach the city walls. The city is eventually taken by storm, and Alexander orders the destruction of Tyre as a warning to other potential rebels. The population is either killed or enslaved (estimates range from 6,000 to 30,000 deaths). The destruction of Tyre eliminates a major Persian naval base and demonstrates Alexander's determination to crush resistance. The causeway built by Alexander remains visible for centuries and eventually becomes a permanent land bridge.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Tyre is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 333–332 BCE. The exact duration and casualty figures are somewhat uncertain, but the general account is consistent across sources. Archaeological evidence from Tyre provides some corroboration for the siege.

events.title: Alexander invades Egypt; recognized as pharaoh
events.description: Following the destruction of Tyre, Alexander advances into Egypt, which had been under Persian control. The Egyptians, who resented Persian rule, welcome Alexander as a liberator. Alexander is crowned pharaoh of Egypt and is recognized as the son of the god Amun-Ra by the Egyptian priesthood. This event is significant for Alexander's self-presentation as a divinely favored leader and for his integration into Egyptian religious and political traditions. Alexander spends several months in Egypt, consolidating his control and establishing himself as the legitimate successor to the pharaohs. He also visits the Oracle of Amun at Siwa, where he is said to have received a prophecy confirming his divine parentage.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's invasion of Egypt and his recognition as pharaoh are well-attested in ancient sources and are dated to 332 BCE. Egyptian temple inscriptions provide some corroboration for his recognition as pharaoh. However, the details of his coronation and his visit to the Oracle of Siwa are somewhat uncertain.
cross_references: 332-003 (Alexander's visit to the Oracle of Siwa)

events.title: Alexander visits the Oracle of Siwa; receives prophecy of divine parentage
events.description: During his stay in Egypt, Alexander travels to the Oracle of Siwa in the western desert to consult the oracle of Amun-Ra. The oracle is said to have confirmed Alexander's divine parentage, pronouncing him the son of Amun-Ra (the Egyptian equivalent of Zeus). This event is significant for Alexander's self-presentation as a divinely favored leader and for his integration into Egyptian religious traditions. The prophecy enhances Alexander's prestige and legitimacy as pharaoh of Egypt. However, the exact details of the oracle's pronouncement are uncertain, and the account may have been embellished in later sources.
key_figures: The priestess of Amun at Siwa
events.certainty_note: The visit to Siwa is attested in ancient sources, but the exact details of the oracle's pronouncement are uncertain. The account may have been embellished in later sources for propaganda purposes. Some modern scholars question whether Alexander actually visited Siwa, though most accept that he did.

events.title: Alexander founds the city of Alexandria in Egypt
events.description: During his stay in Egypt, Alexander founds the city of Alexandria on the Mediterranean coast. The city is designed by the Greek architect Dinocrates and is intended to be a major port and center of Hellenistic culture. Alexandria will become one of the most important cities in the ancient world, serving as the capital of the Ptolemaic Kingdom after Alexander's death. The city is laid out on a grid plan and includes a famous lighthouse (the Pharos) and a great library (the Library of Alexandria). The founding of Alexandria is one of Alexander's most lasting achievements, as the city will remain a major center of learning and culture for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The founding of Alexandria is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 332 BCE. Archaeological evidence from Alexandria provides some corroboration for the city's founding and layout. However, some details about the founding (such as the role of Dinocrates) are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Darius III prepares for final confrontation with Alexander
events.description: While Alexander is consolidating his control of Egypt, Darius III is preparing for a final confrontation with the Macedonian king. Darius III gathers a large army from across the Persian Empire, including troops from Mesopotamia, Persia, and other provinces. He plans to meet Alexander in battle in Mesopotamia, where he hopes to use his numerical superiority to defeat the Macedonian forces. This preparation will lead to the Battle of Gaugamela in 331 BCE, which will be the final major battle between Alexander and Darius III.
events.certainty_note: The preparation of Darius III's army is attested in ancient sources, but the exact details and timing are somewhat uncertain. The sources focus primarily on Alexander's perspective, so information about Persian preparations is limited.

events.title: Qin state continues consolidation during Warring States period
events.region: China (Qin state, central and western China)
events.description: In China, the Qin state continues to consolidate its power during the Warring States period. The Qin implements administrative reforms and military innovations that will eventually allow it to unify China. In 332 BCE, Qin is one of several competing states, but its centralized government and military strength are beginning to set it apart. The Qin will eventually conquer all other states and establish the Qin Dynasty in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Qin rulers of the period (specific ruler in 332 BCE uncertain)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for this period are preserved in later compilations. The exact events of 332 BCE in Qin are not well-documented in surviving sources. The dating of Warring States events is uncertain and may differ by several years depending on the source.

disconfirming_evidence: The siege of Tyre is sometimes portrayed as a quick and decisive victory, but in fact it lasted approximately seven months and was one of the most difficult sieges Alexander undertook. The casualty figures reported in ancient sources are likely exaggerated. The account of Alexander's visit to the Oracle of Siwa is attested in ancient sources, but some modern scholars question whether the visit actually occurred or whether it is a later embellishment. The founding of Alexandria is well-attested, but some details about the city's founding (such as the role of Dinocrates) are uncertain. The account of Alexander's recognition as pharaoh is attested in Egyptian temple inscriptions, but the exact nature of his coronation and his integration into Egyptian religious traditions are somewhat uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 332 BCE is dominated by Greek and Macedonian sources, particularly the accounts of Alexander's campaigns by Arrian, Plutarch, and Diodorus Siculus. These sources are based on earlier histories that are now lost, and they were written centuries after the events they describe. The perspective is decidedly pro-Alexander and Macedonian-centric. Egyptian sources are limited, but temple inscriptions provide some corroboration for Alexander's recognition as pharaoh. The accounts of Alexander's campaigns are often written in a heroic or propagandistic style, emphasizing Alexander's military genius and divine favor. Archaeological evidence from Tyre and Alexandria provides some corroboration for the locations of events, but does not resolve all uncertainties about the exact chronology and details of events.

graph_edges.note: Completion of the siege of Tyre allowed Alexander to invade Egypt
Alexander's invasion of Egypt led to his visit to the Oracle of Siwa
Alexander's invasion of Egypt led to the founding of Alexandria
The siege of Tyre, begun in 333 BCE, was completed in 332 BCE
Darius III's preparation for battle led to the Battle of Gaugamela
